1:
"And it came to pass after these things, that God did tempt Abraham, and said unto him, Abraham: and he said, Behold, here I am." The witnesses note God's testing of Abraham — the crux of "tempt." On "God did tempt Abraham," interpreters expound the word. The puritan Henry and witnesses note that "God did tempt (tried, proved, tested) Abraham" — God did not tempt him to evil (for "God cannot be tempted with evil, neither tempteth he any man," James 1:13), but tried and proved his faith and obedience; the "tempting" here is a testing, a trial of the patriarch's faith (as God proved Job); the great trial of Abraham, to bring forth and display his faith; the testing that distinguishes God's trying of the godly from Satan's tempting to sin.

On "did tempt Abraham," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the trial. Calvin and Gill note that the trial was a real testing — God commanded what would prove Abraham's faith to the uttermost (the offering of Isaac, v.2); the former trials (the call, 12:1; the long wait for the seed) did not supersede this, the greatest; the proving of the patriarch's faith; the trial that, though severe, was for Abraham's good and for a witness to all (Hebrews 11:17).

On "Behold, here I am," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the readiness: Abraham's answer, "Behold, here I am," shows his ready, willing attendance on God's call; the prompt obedience of the servant, present and attentive; the readiness of faith to hear and obey, before he knew the hard command (v.2). The verse displays God's testing of Abraham.

The witnesses agree the substance: God tested (tried, proved) Abraham, who answered, Here I am, ready (Henry, Calvin, Gill, Wesley). The word "tempt" bears a crux: a testing/trying of Abraham's faith and obedience, not a tempting to evil (God tempts no man to sin, James 1:13) — the proving of the godly, as God tried Job (the dominant reading); the trial designed to bring forth and display Abraham's faith (Hebrews 11:17). The verse displays the tempt/test crux. The verse marks the tempt: a test. The verse displays the debate: testing, not tempting to evil. The verse displays the faith: tried. The verse displays the readiness: here I am. The testing displays God proving Abraham's faith. The verse displays the God: trying. The verse displays the Abraham: ready. The verse displays the trial: greatest. God's testing of Abraham displays the trial and a crux: God did tempt Abraham — read as a testing/trying of his faith and obedience, not a tempting to evil (James 1:13), the proving of the godly as God tried Job; Abraham answering, Here I am, ready. The verse displays the tested Abraham, given the hard command (v.2).
2:
"And he said, Take now thy son, thine only son Isaac, whom thou lovest, and get thee into the land of Moriah; and offer him there for a burnt offering upon one of the mountains which I will tell thee of." The witnesses note the command to offer Isaac — the crux of the trial and the type. On "Take now thy son, thine only son Isaac, whom thou lovest," the grammatical voices note the heightening of the trial. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "Take now thy son, thine only son Isaac, whom thou lovest" — the words are heaped up (thy son, thine only son, whom thou lovest, even Isaac) to heighten the severity of the trial; the command touched all that was dearest to Abraham — his son, his only son (by Sarah, the son of promise), the son he loved; the trial pressed to the uttermost; the command to offer the very child of promise, through whom all the blessings hung (21:12).

On "offer him there for a burnt offering," interpreters note the command and the moral question. The reformed Calvin and Gill note that God commanded Abraham to "offer him for a burnt offering" — to sacrifice Isaac; the command, on its face, seemed to contradict God's promise (the seed through Isaac, 21:12) and the law against murder (9:6); yet it was a trial, and God never intended the actual death (he provided the ram, v.13, and Abraham believed God could raise Isaac, Hebrews 11:19); God, the Lord of life, may command what he wills, and the command was to prove Abraham, not to be carried to the death; the moral difficulty resolved in that God stayed the hand and provided the substitute.

On the offering, the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the typology: the offering of the only beloved son is a striking type of the sacrifice of Christ — the Father offering his only-begotten, beloved Son (John 3:16; Romans 8:32, "he that spared not his own Son"); Isaac, the only son whom Abraham loved, offered (in figure) and received back as from the dead (Hebrews 11:19, "in a figure"), foreshadowing the death and resurrection of Christ; the land of Moriah (where the temple would stand, 2 Chronicles 3:1, near Calvary) the place of the offering; the type of the great sacrifice. The verse displays the command to offer Isaac.

The witnesses agree the substance: God commanded Abraham to offer Isaac, his only beloved son, in the land of Moriah (Keil, Calvin, Gill, Henry). The command bears a crux: a supreme trial of faith, God never intending the actual death (he provided the ram, v.13; Abraham believing in the resurrection, Hebrews 11:19), the moral difficulty resolved by the staying of the hand; and the offering of the only beloved son a type of the Father's offering of his only Son, Christ (John 3:16; Romans 8:32), on Moriah. The verse displays the command crux. The verse marks the son: only, beloved. The verse displays the debate: the trial and the typology. The verse displays the offering: a burnt offering. The verse displays the Moriah: the place. The command displays the trial of faith and the type of Christ. The verse displays the Isaac: offered. The verse displays the trial: supreme. The verse displays the type: of Christ. The command to offer Isaac displays the trial and a crux: take thy only son whom thou lovest, and offer him on Moriah — the supreme trial of faith, God never intending the death (the ram provided, v.13; the resurrection believed, Hebrews 11:19), and the offering of the only beloved son a type of the Father's offering of his only Son, Christ (John 3:16; Romans 8:32). The verse displays the commanded Abraham, who obeys promptly (v.3).
3:
"And Abraham rose up early in the morning, and saddled his ass, and took two of his young men with him, and Isaac his son, and clave the wood for the burnt offering, and rose up, and went unto the place of which God had told him." The witnesses note Abraham's prompt obedience to the hard command. On "Abraham rose up early in the morning," the grammatical voices note the promptness. Ellicott and Gill note that "Abraham rose up early in the morning" — the patriarch obeyed without delay, rising early to do the hard thing; the prompt, unquestioning obedience of faith (despite the anguish the command must have caused); the readiness to obey God at once, however grievous; the obedience that "staggered not at the promise... through unbelief" (Romans 4:20).

On "saddled his ass... clave the wood for the burnt offering," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the preparation. Henry and Gill note that Abraham made all the preparations himself — saddled the ass, took the young men and Isaac, clave the wood; the deliberate, methodical obedience; the patriarch attending to every detail of the dreadful task; the steadfastness of his resolve.

On "went unto the place of which God had told him," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the obedience: "went unto the place of which God had told him" — Abraham set out for Moriah (v.2), obeying God's direction; the journey of obedience and faith; the patriarch going, not knowing fully how God would fulfill the promise, yet obeying (Hebrews 11:8, 17-19); the obedience of faith carried out. The verse displays Abraham's prompt obedience.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the promptness — "Abraham rose up early in the morning," the prompt obedience without delay (Romans 4:20, Ellicott, Gill); "saddled his ass... clave the wood," the deliberate preparation (Henry, Gill); "went unto the place of which God had told him," the obedience of faith (Hebrews 11:17-19, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the obedient Abraham. The verse marks the rising: early. The verse displays the preparation: wood, ass, men. The verse displays the journey: to Moriah. The verse displays the obedience: prompt. The obedience displays Abraham obeying the hard command at once. The verse displays the Abraham: rising. The verse displays the wood: cleft. The verse displays the journey: begun. Abraham's prompt obedience to the hard command displays the obedience of faith: Abraham rose early, prepared, and went to the place God told him — the patriarch obeying the dreadful command without delay or question, the steadfast obedience of faith (Hebrews 11:17-19). The verse displays the journeying Abraham, who sees the place afar off (v.4).
4:
"Then on the third day Abraham lifted up his eyes, and saw the place afar off." The brief verse notes Abraham seeing the place of sacrifice on the third day. The grammatical voices note the journey. Ellicott and Gill note that "on the third day Abraham lifted up his eyes, and saw the place afar off" — after three days' journey, Abraham saw the place (Moriah) at a distance; the three days marking the length of the trial (the patriarch's resolve held through three days' anguish); the place of the offering coming into view.

On "the third day," the reformed JFB and witnesses note the trial. JFB and Calvin note that the "third day" marks the prolonged trial — Abraham's faith and obedience tested through three days of journeying with the son he was to offer; the steadfastness of his resolve, not failing through the long ordeal; (some see in the three days a figure of the time between Isaac's being, as it were, given up to death and his being received back, Hebrews 11:19, answering to the three days of Christ).

On "saw the place afar off," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the sight: "saw the place afar off" — Abraham recognized the place God had appointed (Moriah, v.2); the place of the offering in view; the patriarch nearing the dreadful task; the steadfast approach to obey. The verse displays Abraham seeing the place on the third day.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the journey — "on the third day Abraham lifted up his eyes, and saw the place afar off," the place seen after three days (Ellicott, Gill); "the third day," the prolonged trial of faith (Hebrews 11:19, JFB, Calvin); "saw the place afar off," the place of the offering in view (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the journeying Abraham. The verse marks the day: third. The verse displays the place: seen afar. The verse displays the trial: prolonged. The verse displays the resolve: steadfast. The journey displays Abraham nearing the place through the long trial. The verse displays the Abraham: seeing. The verse displays the place: afar off. The verse displays the trial: enduring. Abraham seeing the place of sacrifice on the third day displays the prolonged trial of faith: on the third day Abraham saw the place afar off — the patriarch's resolve held through three days' anguish, the place of the offering coming into view (the three days a figure, some say, of the death and resurrection, Hebrews 11:19). The verse displays the approaching Abraham, leaving the young men (v.5).
5:
"And Abraham said unto his young men, Abide ye here with the ass; and I and the lad will go yonder and worship, and come again to you." The witnesses note Abraham's words of faith — "we will come again." On "Abide ye here with the ass; and I and the lad will go yonder and worship," the grammatical voices note the leaving. Ellicott and Gill note that "Abide ye here with the ass; and I and the lad will go yonder and worship" — Abraham left the young men with the ass and went on alone with Isaac to the place; the patriarch keeping the dreadful task private; calling the offering "worship" (the sacrifice an act of worship and obedience to God); the going up to worship.

On "and come again to you," interpreters note the word of faith. The puritan Henry and witnesses note that Abraham said, "and come again to you" — "I and the lad will... come again"; a remarkable word of faith — Abraham, resolved to offer Isaac, yet expected to come again with him; whether spoken in faith that God would raise Isaac from the dead (Hebrews 11:19, "accounting that God was able to raise him up, even from the dead; from whence also he received him in a figure"), or that God would somehow restore him; the faith that, even in obeying the command to slay, held to the promise (that the seed should be through Isaac, 21:12); the word betraying Abraham's confidence that Isaac would live.

On "we will come again," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the faith: Abraham's "come again to you" is the language of faith — he believed God would fulfill the promise through Isaac, even if he must offer him; the faith that reconciled the command and the promise by trusting God's power to raise the dead (Hebrews 11:19); the patriarch's unshaken faith, that the son of promise would live. The verse displays Abraham's words of faith.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the leaving — "Abide ye here with the ass; and I and the lad will go yonder and worship," Abraham going on alone with Isaac to worship (Ellicott, Gill); "and come again to you," the word of faith, expecting to return with Isaac (Hebrews 11:19, Henry); the faith trusting God to raise/restore Isaac (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the believing Abraham. The verse marks the young men: left behind. The verse displays the worship: to offer. The verse displays the faith: come again. The verse displays the resurrection: believed. The words display Abraham's faith that Isaac would live. The verse displays the Abraham: believing. The verse displays the lad: with him. The verse displays the return: expected. Abraham's words of faith display his unshaken trust: I and the lad will go and worship, and come again to you — the patriarch, resolved to offer Isaac, yet expecting to return with him, believing God could raise the dead (Hebrews 11:19), holding command and promise together in faith. The verse displays the believing Abraham, laying the wood on Isaac (v.6).
6:
"And Abraham took the wood of the burnt offering, and laid it upon Isaac his son; and he took the fire in his hand, and a knife; and they went both of them together." The witnesses note Isaac bearing the wood as they went together. On "Abraham took the wood of the burnt offering, and laid it upon Isaac his son," the grammatical voices note the wood. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Abraham took the wood of the burnt offering, and laid it upon Isaac his son" — Abraham laid the wood for the sacrifice on Isaac to carry; Isaac (a youth, strong enough to bear the wood) carried the wood of his own intended offering; a striking figure — Isaac bearing the wood as Christ bore his cross (John 19:17); the type of the Son carrying the wood of his own sacrifice.

On "he took the fire in his hand, and a knife," the witnesses note the instruments. Gill notes that Abraham carried the fire and the knife (the instruments of the sacrifice) in his own hand; the dreadful means of the offering borne by the father; the patriarch ready to perform the sacrifice.

On "they went both of them together," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the going: "they went both of them together" — father and son went on together to the place; the touching scene — the father bearing the fire and knife, the son the wood, both going to the offering; the unsuspecting Isaac and the resolved Abraham; the type of the Father and the Son together in the work of redemption; the going up to the sacrifice. The verse displays Isaac bearing the wood as they went together.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the wood — "Abraham took the wood... and laid it upon Isaac his son," Isaac bearing the wood of his offering (a type of Christ bearing the cross, John 19:17, Keil, Gill); "he took the fire in his hand, and a knife," the instruments borne by the father (Gill); "they went both of them together," father and son going to the offering (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bearing Isaac. The verse marks the wood: on Isaac. The verse displays the fire: and knife. The verse displays the going: together. The verse displays the type: Christ bearing the cross. The scene displays father and son going to the sacrifice. The verse displays the Isaac: bearing. The verse displays the Abraham: with the fire. The verse displays the going: together. Isaac bearing the wood as they went together displays the touching scene and the type: Abraham laid the wood on Isaac, took the fire and knife, and they went both together — Isaac bearing the wood of his offering (as Christ bore his cross, John 19:17), the father and son going up to the sacrifice. The verse displays the going pair, with Isaac's question (v.7).
7:
"And Isaac spake unto Abraham his father, and said, My father: and he said, Here am I, my son. And he said, Behold the fire and the wood: but where is the lamb for a burnt offering?" The witnesses note Isaac's question about the lamb. On "Isaac spake unto Abraham his father... My father," the grammatical voices note the tender exchange. Gill and Clarke note that "Isaac spake unto Abraham his father, and said, My father: and he said, Here am I, my son" — the touching exchange between father and son; Isaac's address, "My father," and Abraham's tender answer, "Here am I, my son"; the affection between them, heightening the pathos of the scene; the father about to offer the son who called him "father."

On "Behold the fire and the wood: but where is the lamb for a burnt offering?" the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the question. Calvin and Gill note that Isaac asked, "Behold the fire and the wood: but where is the lamb for a burnt offering?" — the lad, seeing the fire and wood but no victim, asked where the lamb was; the innocent, unsuspecting question, piercing the father's heart; Isaac not yet knowing he himself was the intended offering; the question that drew out Abraham's word of faith (v.8).

On the question, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the pathos: Isaac's question — innocent and trusting — must have cut Abraham deeply; the father bearing the dreadful knowledge while the son asked for the lamb; the pathos of the scene; the question answered by Abraham's faith that God would provide (v.8). The verse displays Isaac's question about the lamb.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the exchange — "Isaac spake unto Abraham his father... My father... Here am I, my son," the tender exchange between father and son (Gill, Clarke); "where is the lamb for a burnt offering?" Isaac's innocent question (Calvin, Gill); the pathos, the father bearing the knowledge (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the questioning Isaac. The verse marks the address: my father. The verse displays the question: where is the lamb. The verse displays the innocence: of Isaac. The verse displays the pathos: of the scene. The question displays Isaac's innocent inquiry piercing the father. The verse displays the Isaac: asking. The verse displays the father: answering. The verse displays the lamb: sought. Isaac's question about the lamb displays the pathos of the scene: My father, behold the fire and the wood, but where is the lamb? — the innocent, trusting question of the son who knew not he was the offering, piercing the father's heart, drawing out his word of faith (v.8). The verse displays the questioning Isaac, answered by faith (v.8).
8:
"And Abraham said, My son, God will provide himself a lamb for a burnt offering: so they went both of them together." The witnesses note Abraham's word, "God will provide himself a lamb" — the crux of the faith and the type. On "God will provide himself a lamb for a burnt offering," interpreters expound the word. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note that "God will provide himself a lamb for a burnt offering" — Abraham's answer to Isaac, a word of faith and (as it proved) of prophecy: God himself would provide the offering; Abraham trusted that God would furnish the victim (and God did, the ram, v.13); the unshaken faith of the patriarch, leaving the provision to God; the word reconciling his obedience (offering Isaac) with his trust (that God would provide).

On "God will provide himself a lamb," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the prophecy and the type. Henry and Gill note that Abraham's word was a prophecy — fulfilled immediately in the ram (v.13), and ultimately in Christ, "the Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world" (John 1:29), whom God would provide as the true offering; the word "God will provide himself a lamb" looks beyond the ram to the great Sacrifice, the Lamb whom God himself would furnish (the Father providing the Son, John 3:16; 1 Peter 1:19-20); the type of Christ, the Lamb provided by God; "Jehovah-jireh" (v.14, the LORD will provide).

On "so they went both of them together," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the going: "so they went both of them together" — father and son went on together to the place, Abraham's faith resting in God's provision; the resolved obedience continuing; the going up to the offering, in the faith that God would provide. The verse displays Abraham's word, "God will provide himself a lamb."

The witnesses agree the substance: Abraham answered that God would provide the lamb, and they went on together (Clarke, Henry, Gill, Calvin). The word bears a crux: Abraham's word of faith — trusting God to provide the victim (fulfilled in the ram, v.13); and a prophecy/type of Christ, the Lamb of God whom God himself would provide as the true offering (John 1:29; John 3:16; 1 Peter 1:19-20). The verse displays the provide-a-lamb crux. The verse marks the word: God will provide. The verse displays the debate: faith in the ram, and the type of Christ. The verse displays the lamb: provided. The verse displays the Lamb of God: foreshadowed. The word displays Abraham's faith and the prophecy of Christ. The verse displays the Abraham: believing. The verse displays the lamb: God's. The verse displays the type: Christ. Abraham's word, "God will provide himself a lamb," displays his faith and a crux: God will provide himself a lamb for a burnt offering — Abraham's word of faith (fulfilled in the ram, v.13), and a prophecy/type of Christ, the Lamb of God whom God himself would provide (John 1:29; John 3:16); the unshaken faith resting in God's provision. The verse displays the believing Abraham, who builds the altar and binds Isaac (vv.9-10).
9:
"And they came to the place which God had told him of; and Abraham built an altar there, and laid the wood in order, and bound Isaac his son, and laid him on the altar upon the wood." The witnesses note Abraham building the altar and binding Isaac. On "Abraham built an altar there, and laid the wood in order," the grammatical voices note the preparation. Ellicott and Gill note that "they came to the place which God had told him of; and Abraham built an altar there, and laid the wood in order" — at Moriah, Abraham built the altar and arranged the wood for the offering; the deliberate, resolved obedience, carrying out every step of the dreadful task; the patriarch preparing the sacrifice of his son.

On "bound Isaac his son, and laid him on the altar upon the wood," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the binding (the Akedah). Calvin and Gill note that Abraham "bound Isaac his son, and laid him on the altar upon the wood" — the binding of Isaac (the "Akedah") — the son bound and laid on the altar, ready for the knife; Isaac's submission (he, a strong youth, submitting to be bound by his aged father, a willing victim — a striking type of Christ, who willingly gave himself, John 10:18; Philippians 2:8); the resolved obedience of the father and the submission of the son; the supreme moment of the trial.

On the binding, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the type: the binding and laying of Isaac on the altar — the son a willing, submissive victim — foreshadows Christ, who was bound and offered, the willing Lamb (Isaiah 53:7; Acts 8:32); the obedience of Isaac (submitting to his father) a figure of the obedience of Christ (to the Father); the type of the great Sacrifice. The verse displays Abraham building the altar and binding Isaac.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the preparation — "Abraham built an altar there, and laid the wood in order," the resolved preparation of the sacrifice (Ellicott, Gill); "bound Isaac his son, and laid him on the altar," the binding (Akedah), Isaac the willing victim (a type of Christ, John 10:18, Calvin, Gill); the submission of Isaac foreshadowing Christ (Isaiah 53:7, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the binding Abraham. The verse marks the altar: built. The verse displays the wood: in order. The verse displays the Isaac: bound. The verse displays the type: Christ. The binding displays Abraham preparing and binding the willing Isaac. The verse displays the altar: raised. The verse displays the Isaac: laid on it. The verse displays the submission: shown. Abraham building the altar and binding Isaac display the supreme moment and the type: Abraham built the altar, laid the wood, bound Isaac, and laid him on it — the resolved obedience of the father and the submission of the son (the willing victim, a type of Christ, Isaiah 53:7; Philippians 2:8). The verse displays the bound Isaac, the knife raised (v.10).
10:
"And Abraham stretched forth his hand, and took the knife to slay his son." The very brief verse notes Abraham reaching for the knife to slay Isaac. The grammatical voices note the supreme act. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Abraham stretched forth his hand, and took the knife to slay his son" — Abraham, at the last moment, reached out his hand and took the knife, ready to slay Isaac; the supreme test of obedience, carried to the very point of the act; the patriarch's faith and obedience proved to the uttermost — he would have done it, but for God's intervention (v.11); the resolved hand raised over the bound son.

On "stretched forth his hand, and took the knife," the reformed and witnesses note the resolve. Gill notes that Abraham's stretching forth his hand to take the knife shows the completeness of his obedience — he held nothing back, but was about to slay his son; the faith that would obey to the death; the trial at its height.

On "to slay his son," the grammatical-historical note the proof: "to slay his son" — Abraham was on the very point of slaying Isaac; the obedience proved to the last; God reckoned the act as done in Abraham's resolved will (v.12, "thou hast not withheld thy son"; Hebrews 11:17, "offered up Isaac"); the supreme moment, the hand raised, before God stayed it (v.11). The verse displays Abraham reaching for the knife to slay Isaac.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the act — "Abraham stretched forth his hand, and took the knife to slay his son," Abraham at the point of slaying Isaac (Keil, Gill); the completeness of his obedience, holding nothing back (Gill); "to slay his son," the obedience proved to the last (v.12; Hebrews 11:17, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the resolved Abraham. The verse marks the hand: stretched forth. The verse displays the knife: taken. The verse displays the slaying: about to be. The verse displays the obedience: complete. The act displays Abraham proved to the uttermost. The verse displays the Abraham: reaching. The verse displays the knife: raised. The verse displays the obedience: utmost. Abraham reaching for the knife to slay Isaac displays the obedience proved to the uttermost: Abraham stretched forth his hand and took the knife to slay his son — the patriarch at the very point of the act, his faith and obedience complete (reckoned as done, Hebrews 11:17), before God stayed his hand. The verse displays the raised hand, arrested by the angel (v.11).
11:
"And the angel of the LORD called unto him out of heaven, and said, Abraham, Abraham: and he said, Here am I." The witnesses note the angel of the LORD staying Abraham's hand. On "the angel of the LORD called unto him out of heaven," the grammatical voices note the intervention. Ellicott and Gill note that "the angel of the LORD called unto him out of heaven, and said, Abraham, Abraham" — at the very moment of the act (v.10), the Angel of the LORD called from heaven to stop Abraham; the timely intervention; the Angel (who speaks as God, v.12, 16, "thou hast not withheld thy son from me"; "by myself have I sworn") is, by the dominant reading, a divine Person (the Angel of the LORD, the pre-incarnate Son, as 16:7-13); God himself staying the hand.

On "Abraham, Abraham," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the urgent call. Henry and Gill note the doubled name — "Abraham, Abraham" — the urgent, emphatic call (as to Moses, Exodus 3:4; Samuel, 1 Samuel 3:10); the urgency of the moment, the hand about to fall; the affectionate, arresting call to stay the patriarch.

On "Here am I," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the readiness: Abraham's answer, "Here am I" (as v.1), shows his ready attention even at the supreme moment; the obedient servant, attentive to God's voice; the patriarch arrested in the act, ready to hear God's word (v.12). The verse displays the angel of the LORD staying Abraham's hand.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the intervention — "the angel of the LORD called unto him out of heaven," the Angel staying Abraham at the moment of the act (the Angel a divine Person, 16:7-13; v.12, Ellicott, Gill); "Abraham, Abraham," the urgent, doubled call (Exodus 3:4, Henry, Gill); "Here am I," Abraham's ready attention (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the arrested Abraham. The verse marks the angel: from heaven. The verse displays the call: Abraham, Abraham. The verse displays the staying: of the hand. The verse displays the readiness: here am I. The intervention displays God staying the hand at the supreme moment. The verse displays the Angel: calling. The verse displays the Abraham: arrested. The verse displays the obedience: ready. The angel of the LORD staying Abraham's hand displays the timely intervention: the angel of the LORD called from heaven, Abraham, Abraham — the Angel (a divine Person, v.12, 16) staying the patriarch at the very moment of the act, Abraham answering, Here am I. The verse displays the stayed Abraham, his faith approved (v.12).
12:
"And he said, Lay not thine hand upon the lad, neither do thou any thing unto him: for now I know that thou fearest God, seeing thou hast not withheld thy son, thine only son from me." The witnesses note God staying the hand and approving Abraham's fear. On "Lay not thine hand upon the lad, neither do thou any thing unto him," the grammatical voices note the staying. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Lay not thine hand upon the lad, neither do thou any thing unto him" — God forbade Abraham to harm Isaac; the actual sacrifice was not required (it was the trial, not the death, that God sought); the staying of the hand, the son spared; God, having proved Abraham, would not have the death.

On "for now I know that thou fearest God," interpreters note the divine "knowing." The reformed Calvin and Gill note that "now I know that thou fearest God" — God speaks after the manner of men: not that God learned anything he did not know (he knows all hearts), but that Abraham's fear of God was now proved, displayed, made evident by the act; the "knowing" is the manifestation of what God knew, shown to Abraham and to all; the trial bringing forth and approving Abraham's faith and fear of God (the fear of God meaning his reverent, obedient devotion).

On "seeing thou hast not withheld thy son, thine only son from me," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the proof: "thou hast not withheld thy son, thine only son from me" — Abraham's not withholding Isaac (his readiness to offer even his only son) was the proof of his fear and faith; the act reckoned as done (Hebrews 11:17, "offered up Isaac"); the supreme proof of devotion — withholding nothing, not even the dearest, from God; the type, again, of the Father who "spared not his own Son" (Romans 8:32). The verse displays God staying the hand and approving Abraham's fear.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the staying — "Lay not thine hand upon the lad," God forbidding the harm, the son spared (Keil, Gill); "now I know that thou fearest God," Abraham's fear proved and displayed (an anthropomorphism, Calvin, Gill); "thou hast not withheld thy son, thine only son from me," the proof of devotion (Hebrews 11:17; Romans 8:32, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the approved Abraham. The verse marks the staying: lay not thine hand. The verse displays the knowing: now I know. The verse displays the fear: proved. The verse displays the not-withholding: of Isaac. The word displays God sparing Isaac and approving Abraham's faith. The verse displays the God: staying. The verse displays the Abraham: approved. The verse displays the fear: shown. God staying the hand and approving Abraham's fear display the issue of the trial: lay not thine hand on the lad, for now I know that thou fearest God, seeing thou hast not withheld thy only son — the son spared, Abraham's fear and faith proved and displayed (the act reckoned as done, Hebrews 11:17), withholding nothing from God (as the Father spared not his Son, Romans 8:32). The verse displays the approved Abraham, who sees the ram (v.13).
13:
"And Abraham lifted up his eyes, and looked, and behold behind him a ram caught in a thicket by his horns: and Abraham went and took the ram, and offered him up for a burnt offering in the stead of his son." The witnesses note the ram provided and offered in Isaac's stead. On "behold behind him a ram caught in a thicket by his horns," the grammatical voices note the provision. Ellicott and Gill note that "Abraham lifted up his eyes, and looked, and behold behind him a ram caught in a thicket by his horns" — God provided a ram, caught by its horns in a thicket, for the offering; the fulfillment of Abraham's word (v.8, "God will provide himself a lamb"); the substitute provided by God at the very moment; the ram caught and ready.

On "Abraham went and took the ram, and offered him up for a burnt offering," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the offering. Calvin and Gill note that Abraham "offered him up for a burnt offering in the stead of his son" — the ram was offered in Isaac's place; the substitution — the ram dying instead of Isaac; the burnt offering completed with the substitute; the son spared, the ram offered.

On "in the stead of his son," the puritan and grammatical-historical note the type: the ram offered "in the stead of his son" is a striking figure of substitution — the victim dying in the place of the condemned; the type of Christ, who died in the stead of sinners (the substitutionary sacrifice, Isaiah 53:5-6; 1 Peter 3:18, "the just for the unjust"); Isaac (the bound son) spared, and the ram (the provided victim) offered in his place — the gospel of substitution foreshadowed; the Lamb of God dying in the sinner's stead. The verse displays the ram provided and offered in Isaac's stead.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the provision — "behold behind him a ram caught in a thicket by his horns," God providing the ram (the word of v.8 fulfilled, Ellicott, Gill); "offered him up for a burnt offering in the stead of his son," the ram offered in Isaac's place (Calvin, Gill); the substitution a type of Christ dying in the sinner's stead (Isaiah 53:5-6; 1 Peter 3:18, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the provided ram. The verse marks the ram: in the thicket. The verse displays the offering: in Isaac's stead. The verse displays the substitution: the ram for the son. The verse displays the type: Christ. The provision displays God providing the substitute. The verse displays the ram: caught. The verse displays the offering: made. The verse displays the substitution: shown. The ram provided and offered in Isaac's stead displays the substitution and the type: a ram caught in a thicket, offered in the stead of his son — God providing the substitute (v.8 fulfilled), the ram dying in Isaac's place, the type of Christ who died in the sinner's stead (Isaiah 53:5-6; 1 Peter 3:18). The verse displays the offered ram, the place named Jehovah-jireh (v.14).
14:
"And Abraham called the name of that place Jehovahjireh: as it is said to this day, In the mount of the LORD it shall be seen." The witnesses note the naming of Jehovah-jireh — the crux of the difficult phrase. On "Abraham called the name of that place Jehovahjireh," the grammatical voices note the name. Ellicott and Gill note that "Abraham called the name of that place Jehovahjireh (the LORD will provide, or the LORD will see/be seen)" — Abraham named the place Jehovah-jireh, commemorating God's provision of the ram (v.13) in answer to his word of faith (v.8, "God will provide"); the name a memorial of the deliverance and the provision; "jireh" (from raah, to see/provide) — "the LORD will see (provide)."

On "In the mount of the LORD it shall be seen," interpreters expound the difficult proverb. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the variant renderings: "as it is said to this day, In the mount of the LORD it shall be seen" — the phrase became a proverb; variously rendered: (a) "In the mount of the LORD it shall be seen (or provided)" — that is, in the place of difficulty, God will be seen to provide (as he had provided the ram); the proverb of trust, that God provides in the extremity; (b) "In the mount the LORD will be seen (will appear)" — pointing to God's appearing/manifestation on the mount (and, by some, to the future — Moriah, where the temple would stand, 2 Chronicles 3:1, and near Calvary, where the great provision, Christ, would be seen); (c) the margin "The LORD will see," "The LORD will provide" — God's provision and foresight. The dominant sense — God provides (and is seen to provide) in the mount, in the place of need.

On the name, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the memorial and the prophecy: Jehovah-jireh, "the LORD will provide," is the memorial of God's provision (the ram), and (as the proverb suggests) a standing testimony that God provides for his people in their extremity; some see a prophecy — that in the mount of the LORD (Moriah/Zion, Calvary) the great provision (Christ, the Lamb) would be seen; "as he has provided, so he will provide... and will send his only Son." The verse displays the naming of Jehovah-jireh.

The witnesses agree the substance: Abraham named the place Jehovah-jireh ("the LORD will provide"), and the proverb "In the mount of the LORD it shall be seen/provided" arose (Ellicott, Gill, Clarke, Calvin). The phrase bears a crux of the difficult Hebrew: Jehovah-jireh = "the LORD will provide" (or "will see/be seen"); and "In the mount of the LORD it shall be seen" variously rendered — God will be seen to provide in the place of need (the proverb of trust), or God will appear/be seen in the mount (pointing, by some, to the future provision, Christ, on Moriah/Calvary). The verse displays the Jehovah-jireh crux. The verse marks the name: Jehovah-jireh. The verse displays the debate: the LORD will provide, or will be seen; the proverb. The verse displays the provision: God's. The verse displays the prophecy: of the mount. The naming displays the memorial of God's provision. The verse displays the Jehovah-jireh: named. The verse displays the provision: remembered. The verse displays the mount: of the LORD. The naming of Jehovah-jireh displays the memorial and a crux: Abraham called the place Jehovah-jireh, and the proverb arose, In the mount of the LORD it shall be seen — read as "the LORD will provide" (the ram, and ever in the place of need), or "the LORD will be seen/appear in the mount" (pointing, by some, to the future provision, Christ, on Moriah/Calvary). The verse displays the named place, with the confirming oath (vv.15-18).
15:
"And the angel of the LORD called unto Abraham out of heaven the second time," The very brief verse notes the angel of the LORD calling Abraham a second time. The grammatical voices note the renewed call. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the angel of the LORD called unto Abraham out of heaven the second time" — the Angel of the LORD (who had stayed his hand, v.11) called again from heaven, now to pronounce the great confirming oath of the covenant (vv.16-18); the second call, after the offering of the ram (v.13); the Angel (a divine Person, swearing by himself, v.16) renewing the promise with an oath.

On "the second time," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the renewal. Henry and Gill note that this was the "second time" the Angel called — the first to stay the hand (v.11), the second to bless and confirm; the renewed communication; the reward of Abraham's obedience now to be declared (vv.16-18); the confirming of the covenant with an oath.

On the call, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the occasion: the second call introduces the solemn oath by which God confirmed his covenant promises to Abraham (vv.16-18), in reward for his obedience; the Angel of the LORD, the divine Person, swearing by himself (v.16); the great confirming oath of the Abrahamic covenant. The verse displays the angel calling Abraham a second time.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the call — "the angel of the LORD called unto Abraham out of heaven the second time," the Angel calling again to confirm the covenant (vv.16-18, Keil, Gill); "the second time," the renewal, the reward of obedience to be declared (Henry, Gill); the introduction to the confirming oath (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the calling Angel. The verse marks the call: second time. The verse displays the Angel: from heaven. The verse displays the oath: to come. The verse displays the reward: of obedience. The call displays the Angel renewing the promise with an oath. The verse displays the Angel: calling. The verse displays the second: time. The verse displays the covenant: confirmed. The angel of the LORD calling Abraham a second time displays the renewal of the promise: the Angel called the second time — now to pronounce the great confirming oath of the covenant (vv.16-18), in reward for Abraham's obedience, the divine Angel swearing by himself. The verse displays the calling Angel, swearing by himself (v.16).
16:
"And said, By myself have I sworn, saith the LORD, for because thou hast done this thing, and hast not withheld thy son, thine only son:" The witnesses note God's confirming the covenant with an oath. On "By myself have I sworn, saith the LORD," the grammatical voices note the oath. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "By myself have I sworn, saith the LORD" — God confirmed his covenant promises with an oath, swearing by himself (there being none greater to swear by, Hebrews 6:13, "because he could swear by no greater, he sware by himself"); the solemn, unchangeable oath of God; the two immutable things (the promise and the oath) by which God confirmed his word (Hebrews 6:17-18); the great confirming oath of the Abrahamic covenant.

On "saith the LORD," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the divine word. Wesley and Gill note that the Angel of the LORD speaks as the LORD ("saith the LORD") — swearing by himself; a token (with v.11-12) that the Angel is a divine Person (the pre-incarnate Son); God himself confirming his promise with an oath.

On "because thou hast done this thing, and hast not withheld thy son, thine only son," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the ground: "because thou hast done this thing, and hast not withheld thy son, thine only son" — the oath was given in reward for Abraham's obedience (his offering of Isaac); not that the promise rested on Abraham's works (it was of grace, 15:6), but that his obedience, the fruit of faith, was crowned and rewarded (James 2:21-23, "was not Abraham our father justified by works, when he had offered Isaac... and the scripture was fulfilled... Abraham believed God"); the obedience of faith rewarded; the not-withholding of the only son the occasion of the confirming oath. The verse displays God's confirming the covenant with an oath.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the oath — "By myself have I sworn, saith the LORD," God confirming his promise with an oath, swearing by himself (Hebrews 6:13, Keil, Gill); the Angel speaking as the LORD (Wesley, Gill); "because thou hast done this thing... hast not withheld thy son," the oath rewarding Abraham's obedience (James 2:21-23, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the swearing LORD. The verse marks the oath: by myself. The verse displays the LORD: swearing. The verse displays the obedience: rewarded. The verse displays the not-withholding: of Isaac. The oath displays God confirming his covenant with an oath. The verse displays the LORD: sworn. The verse displays the promise: confirmed. The verse displays the obedience: crowned. God's confirming the covenant with an oath displays the great confirmation: by myself have I sworn, because thou hast not withheld thy only son — God confirming his promise with an oath, swearing by himself (Hebrews 6:13), rewarding Abraham's obedience of faith (James 2:21-23). The verse displays the swearing LORD, blessing and multiplying (vv.17-18).
17:
"That in blessing I will bless thee, and in multiplying I will multiply thy seed as the stars of the heaven, and as the sand which is upon the sea shore; and thy seed shall possess the gate of his enemies;" The witnesses note the promise of blessing and a numberless seed. On "in blessing I will bless thee, and in multiplying I will multiply thy seed," the grammatical voices note the emphatic promise. Gill and Clarke note that "in blessing I will bless thee, and in multiplying I will multiply thy seed" — the doubled Hebrew ("in blessing... bless," "in multiplying... multiply") marks the certainty and abundance of the promise; God would surely and abundantly bless Abraham and multiply his seed; the confirming of the blessing and the seed-promise with the oath.

On "as the stars of the heaven, and as the sand which is upon the sea shore," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the multitude. Calvin and Gill note the double figure — "as the stars of the heaven, and as the sand... upon the sea shore" — the seed of Abraham numberless, as the stars (15:5) and the sand; the vast multitude of his descendants (natural, Israel; and spiritual, all the faithful, Romans 4:16-18; Galatians 3:7, 29); the immense fulfillment of the promise.

On "thy seed shall possess the gate of his enemies," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the victory: "thy seed shall possess the gate of his enemies" — Abraham's seed would possess (conquer, hold) the gate (the strength, the cities) of their enemies; the victory and dominion of the seed (Israel over the Canaanites; and, supremely, Christ and his people over their spiritual enemies — sin, death, hell, the gates of which shall not prevail, Matthew 16:18); the triumph of the seed. The verse displays the promise of blessing and a numberless seed.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the promise — "in blessing I will bless thee, and in multiplying I will multiply thy seed," the emphatic, certain promise of blessing and seed (Gill, Clarke); "as the stars... and as the sand," the numberless seed (15:5; Romans 4:16-18, Calvin, Gill); "thy seed shall possess the gate of his enemies," the victory of the seed (Matthew 16:18, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the promised seed. The verse marks the blessing: doubled. The verse displays the seed: as stars and sand. The verse displays the victory: the gate of enemies. The verse displays the multitude: numberless. The promise displays God's abundant blessing and numberless seed. The verse displays the blessing: sure. The verse displays the seed: countless. The verse displays the victory: promised. The promise of blessing and a numberless seed displays the confirmed covenant: in blessing I will bless thee, and multiply thy seed as the stars and the sand, and thy seed shall possess the gate of his enemies — the abundant blessing, the numberless seed (15:5; Romans 4:16-18), and the victory of the seed (Matthew 16:18). The verse displays the blessed seed, in whom all nations are blessed (v.18).
18:
"And in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed; because thou hast obeyed my voice." The witnesses note the Gospel promise — all nations blessed in Abraham's seed. On "in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed," the grammatical voices note the great promise. Gill and Clarke note that "in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed" — the great gospel promise (12:3; 18:18) confirmed with the oath: all nations would be blessed in Abraham's seed; Paul declares the "seed" to be Christ — "to thy seed, which is Christ" (Galatians 3:16); in Christ, of Abraham's line, all nations (Jew and Gentile) would be blessed with justification and salvation; the Gospel promise, the heart of the covenant, sworn to Abraham.

On "thy seed," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note Christ. Calvin and Gill note that "thy seed" is, supremely, Christ (Galatians 3:16) — the promised Seed, the Messiah, in whom the blessing comes to all nations; the Gospel preached to Abraham (Galatians 3:8); the blessing of Abraham coming on the Gentiles through Christ (Galatians 3:14); the universal blessing in the one Seed.

On "because thou hast obeyed my voice," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the obedience: "because thou hast obeyed my voice" — the oath and the renewed promise were given in connection with Abraham's obedience (the offering of Isaac); not that the promise was earned (it was of grace), but that Abraham's obedience of faith was crowned, and the covenant confirmed to the obedient patriarch (James 2:21-23); the reward of the faith that obeys. The verse displays the Gospel promise.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the promise — "in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed," the gospel promise confirmed (12:3; Galatians 3:16, Gill, Clarke); "thy seed" supremely Christ, in whom all nations blessed (Galatians 3:8, 14, 16, Calvin, Gill); "because thou hast obeyed my voice," the reward of the obedience of faith (James 2:21-23, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the Gospel promise. The verse marks the seed: Christ. The verse displays the nations: all blessed. The verse displays the Gospel: confirmed. The verse displays the obedience: rewarded. The promise displays the universal blessing in Abraham's seed, Christ. The verse displays the seed: blessed. The verse displays the nations: in him. The verse displays the Gospel: sworn. The Gospel promise displays the universal blessing in Abraham's seed: in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed — the gospel promise (12:3) confirmed with the oath, the seed supremely Christ (Galatians 3:16), in whom all nations are blessed with salvation; rewarded to Abraham's obedience of faith. The verse displays the blessed nations, as Abraham returns to Beersheba (v.19).
19:
"So Abraham returned unto his young men, and they rose up and went together to Beersheba; and Abraham dwelt at Beersheba." The brief verse notes Abraham returning to Beersheba after the trial. The grammatical voices note the return. Gill and Calvin note that "Abraham returned unto his young men, and they rose up and went together to Beersheba; and Abraham dwelt at Beersheba" — the trial over (the son spared, the covenant confirmed), Abraham returned to the young men (left at the foot of the mount, v.5) with Isaac, and went back to Beersheba (his dwelling, 21:33); the return in peace, the great trial accomplished; the patriarch dwelling again at Beersheba.

On "they rose up and went together to Beersheba," the witnesses note the return with Isaac. Gill notes that Abraham returned with Isaac (the "come again to you," v.5, fulfilled); the son restored (as from the dead, Hebrews 11:19); the company reunited; the journey home in peace.

On "Abraham dwelt at Beersheba," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the dwelling: "Abraham dwelt at Beersheba" — the patriarch resumed his dwelling at Beersheba (21:31-33); the peaceful settling after the trial; the great test past, Abraham at rest in his place; the close of the Akedah; the patriarch blessed and at peace. The verse displays Abraham returning to Beersheba after the trial.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the return — "Abraham returned unto his young men... and went together to Beersheba," the return after the trial, with Isaac (v.5, Gill, Calvin); the son restored, "come again" fulfilled (Hebrews 11:19, Gill); "Abraham dwelt at Beersheba," the peaceful dwelling after the trial (21:33, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the returning Abraham. The verse marks the return: to the young men. The verse displays the journey: to Beersheba. The verse displays the Isaac: restored. The verse displays the dwelling: Beersheba. The return displays Abraham home in peace after the trial. The verse displays the Abraham: returning. The verse displays the trial: past. The verse displays the peace: resumed. Abraham returning to Beersheba after the trial displays the peaceful close of the Akedah: Abraham returned to the young men and went to Beersheba, and dwelt there — the great trial over, the son restored (v.5; Hebrews 11:19), the patriarch home in peace. The verse displays the dwelling Abraham, before the news of Nahor's family (v.20).
20:
"And it came to pass after these things, that it was told Abraham, saying, Behold, Milcah, she hath also born children unto thy brother Nahor;" The brief verse notes the news of the children of Nahor. The grammatical voices note the report. Ellicott and Gill note that "it was told Abraham... Behold, Milcah, she hath also born children unto thy brother Nahor" — Abraham received news of the family of his brother Nahor (who had remained in Mesopotamia, 11:27-29); Milcah (Nahor's wife) had borne him children; the report of the growing family of Nahor; the genealogy that prepares for Rebekah (v.23), Isaac's future wife.

On "thy brother Nahor," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the family. Henry and Gill note that the news concerned Nahor, Abraham's brother (11:26-29), and his children; the family left behind in Haran; the connection maintained between Abraham and his kindred; the report bringing word of the home family.

On the news, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the purpose: the genealogy of Nahor's children (vv.20-24) is given chiefly to introduce Rebekah (v.23), the daughter of Bethuel (Nahor's son), who would become Isaac's wife (ch. 24); the providence preparing the bride for the son of promise; the news that sets the stage for the next great event. The verse displays the news of the children of Nahor.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the report — "it was told Abraham... Milcah... hath also born children unto thy brother Nahor," the news of Nahor's family (Ellicott, Gill); "thy brother Nahor," Abraham's brother and his children (11:26-29, Henry, Gill); the genealogy preparing for Rebekah (v.23; ch. 24, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the reported family. The verse marks the news: of Nahor. The verse displays the Milcah: bore children. The verse displays the family: Nahor's. The verse displays the Rebekah: to come. The report displays the news of Abraham's kindred. The verse displays the Nahor: his children. The verse displays the family: growing. The verse displays the bride: prepared. The news of the children of Nahor displays the providence preparing for Rebekah: Milcah bore children to Nahor — the report of Abraham's brother's family, the genealogy (vv.20-24) preparing for Rebekah (v.23), Isaac's future wife (ch. 24). The verse displays the family of Nahor, listed (vv.21-24).
21:
"Huz his firstborn, and Buz his brother, and Kemuel the father of Aram," The very brief verse notes the first of Nahor's sons. The grammatical voices note the names. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Huz his firstborn, and Buz his brother, and Kemuel the father of Aram" — the first three of Nahor's sons by Milcah: Huz (the firstborn, perhaps connected with the land of Uz, Job 1:1), Buz (perhaps connected with Buz, the kindred of Elihu, Job 32:2; Jeremiah 25:23), and Kemuel (the father of Aram, an Aramaean line); the sons of Nahor heading various peoples.

On the names, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the peoples. Clarke and Ellicott note that the sons of Nahor became the heads of various Aramaean and Arabian tribes (Uz, Buz, Aram); the kindred of Abraham spreading into peoples; the families of the brother of the patriarch; the genealogy tracing Nahor's line.

On the sons, the grammatical-historical note the line: the sons of Nahor (vv.21-24) are listed, leading to Bethuel and Rebekah (v.23); the genealogy of Abraham's kindred; the families related to the line of promise (from whom Isaac's wife, and later Jacob's wives, would come); the providence in the kindred of Abraham. The verse displays the first of Nahor's sons.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the names — "Huz his firstborn, and Buz his brother, and Kemuel the father of Aram," the first sons of Nahor (Keil, Gill); the sons heading various peoples (Uz, Buz, Aram, Clarke, Ellicott); the genealogy leading to Bethuel and Rebekah (v.23, grammatical-historical). The verse displays Nahor's sons. The verse marks the sons: Huz, Buz, Kemuel. The verse displays the firstborn: Huz. The verse displays the Aram: through Kemuel. The verse displays the line: of Nahor. The genealogy displays the sons of Nahor heading peoples. The verse displays the Nahor: his sons. The verse displays the names: listed. The verse displays the line: traced. The first of Nahor's sons display the kindred of Abraham: Huz the firstborn, Buz, and Kemuel the father of Aram — the sons of Nahor, heading various peoples, the genealogy leading toward Bethuel and Rebekah (v.23). The verse displays Nahor's sons, continued (vv.22-23).
22:
"And Chesed, and Hazo, and Pildash, and Jidlaph, and Bethuel." The very brief verse notes more of Nahor's sons, including Bethuel. The grammatical voices note the names. Gill and JFB note that "Chesed, and Hazo, and Pildash, and Jidlaph, and Bethuel" — more of the sons of Nahor by Milcah: Chesed (perhaps connected with the Chasdim/Chaldeans), Hazo, Pildash, Jidlaph, and Bethuel (named last, but the most significant for the history, being the father of Rebekah, v.23); the further sons of Nahor.

On "Chesed," the witnesses note the names. Gill and Ellicott note that the sons (Chesed, Hazo, Pildash, Jidlaph) are heads of further families/tribes of Abraham's kindred; the genealogy listing Nahor's eight sons by Milcah (v.23); the families of the patriarch's brother.

On "Bethuel," the grammatical-historical note the significance: "Bethuel" — named last, yet the most important for the sacred history, being the father of Rebekah (v.23), Isaac's future wife (and of Laban, the father of Leah and Rachel); the line through which the brides of the patriarchs would come; the providence preparing the family of the wife of the son of promise. The verse displays more of Nahor's sons, including Bethuel.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the names — "Chesed, and Hazo, and Pildash, and Jidlaph, and Bethuel," more of Nahor's sons (Gill, JFB); the heads of further families (Ellicott, Gill); "Bethuel," the father of Rebekah, the significant name (v.23, grammatical-historical). The verse displays Nahor's sons. The verse marks the sons: Chesed and others. The verse displays the Bethuel: father of Rebekah. The verse displays the family: Nahor's. The verse displays the line: to Rebekah. The genealogy displays more of Nahor's sons, including Bethuel. The verse displays the Nahor: his sons. The verse displays the Bethuel: named. The verse displays the line: traced. More of Nahor's sons, including Bethuel, display the kindred and the providence: Chesed, Hazo, Pildash, Jidlaph, and Bethuel — the further sons of Nahor, Bethuel the father of Rebekah (v.23), through whom Isaac's wife would come. The verse displays Bethuel, the father of Rebekah (v.23).
23:
"And Bethuel begat Rebekah: these eight Milcah did bear to Nahor, Abraham's brother." The brief verse notes Bethuel begetting Rebekah, the future wife of Isaac. The grammatical voices note the birth. Gill and Clarke note that "Bethuel begat Rebekah: these eight Milcah did bear to Nahor, Abraham's brother" — Bethuel (Nahor's son) begat Rebekah; the eight sons Milcah bore to Nahor are summed up; the notable note — Rebekah, the daughter of Bethuel, who would become Isaac's wife (ch. 24); the purpose of the genealogy revealed in Rebekah.

On "Bethuel begat Rebekah," the puritan and witnesses note the providence. Gill notes that the chief point of the genealogy is Rebekah — the granddaughter of Nahor, the future wife of Isaac; the providence preparing the bride for the son of promise; the family from which Isaac's wife (and later Jacob's wives) would come; the connection of the line of promise with the kindred of Abraham.

On "these eight Milcah did bear to Nahor," the grammatical-historical note the family: "these eight Milcah did bear to Nahor, Abraham's brother" — the eight sons of Nahor by his wife Milcah (vv.21-23); the family of Abraham's brother; the genealogy of the kindred, given to introduce Rebekah; the providence in the family of Nahor preparing for the marriage of Isaac (ch. 24). The verse displays Bethuel begetting Rebekah.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the birth — "Bethuel begat Rebekah," Rebekah the daughter of Bethuel, Isaac's future wife (ch. 24, Gill, Clarke); the providence preparing the bride (Gill); "these eight Milcah did bear to Nahor," the family of Nahor (vv.21-23, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the born Rebekah. The verse marks the Bethuel: begat Rebekah. The verse displays the Rebekah: the bride. The verse displays the sons: eight. The verse displays the providence: preparing. The birth displays Rebekah, the future wife of Isaac, born. The verse displays the Rebekah: named. The verse displays the family: Nahor's. The verse displays the bride: prepared. Bethuel begetting Rebekah displays the providence preparing the bride: Bethuel begat Rebekah, of the eight Milcah bore to Nahor — Rebekah, the granddaughter of Abraham's brother, the future wife of Isaac (ch. 24), the providence preparing the bride for the son of promise. The verse displays the born Rebekah, with Nahor's concubine's sons (v.24).
24:
"And his concubine, whose name was Reumah, she bare also Tebah, and Gaham, and Thahash, and Maachah." The very brief verse notes the sons of Nahor's concubine. The grammatical voices note the names. Gill and Clarke note that "his concubine, whose name was Reumah, she bare also Tebah, and Gaham, and Thahash, and Maachah" — Nahor had, besides his sons by Milcah (vv.21-23), four sons by his concubine Reumah: Tebah, Gaham, Thahash, and Maachah; the further offspring of Nahor; the heads of further tribes/families of Abraham's kindred.

On "his concubine... Reumah," the witnesses note the secondary wife. Gill notes that Reumah was Nahor's concubine (secondary wife); the sons by her, like those by Milcah, became heads of families/tribes; the full account of Nahor's offspring; the kindred of Abraham spreading into many peoples.

On the sons, the grammatical-historical note the close: "Tebah, and Gaham, and Thahash, and Maachah" — the sons of the concubine, heads of various Aramaean/Arabian tribes; the completing of the genealogy of Nahor (vv.20-24); the family of Abraham's brother fully recorded; the close of the chapter, having introduced Rebekah (v.23) for the coming marriage of Isaac (ch. 24). The verse displays the sons of Nahor's concubine.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the names — "his concubine, whose name was Reumah, she bare also Tebah, and Gaham, and Thahash, and Maachah," the sons of Nahor's concubine (Gill, Clarke); Reumah the concubine, her sons heads of families (Gill); the completing of Nahor's genealogy (vv.20-24, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the concubine's sons. The verse marks the concubine: Reumah. The verse displays the sons: four. The verse displays the family: Nahor's. The verse displays the genealogy: completed. The list displays the further offspring of Nahor. The verse displays the Reumah: her sons. The verse displays the family: full. The verse displays the genealogy: closed. The sons of Nahor's concubine display the completing of the kindred's genealogy: Reumah bore Tebah, Gaham, Thahash, and Maachah — the sons of Nahor's concubine, heads of further tribes, completing the genealogy of Abraham's brother (vv.20-24), which had introduced Rebekah (v.23). The verse closes the chapter, before the death of Sarah (ch.23).
